articles.author: Brian P. D. Hannon
articles.description: U.S. President Donald Trump pledged the U.S. would “massively blow up the entirety” of the world’s largest gas field if Iran attacks Qatar again. The Iranian attack was in retaliation for an Israeli attack on the South Pars field earlier Wednesday.
articles.title: Live updates: Trump threatens to strike South Pars gas field if Iran attacks Qatar again

articles.author: Sam Metz
articles.description: Palestinians are mourning after an overnight airstrike hit a beauty salon outside Hebron. The Wednesday night strike killed four Palestinian women and injured more than a dozen in the first fatal strike in the occupied West Bank since the start of the Iran war. The Palestinian Red Crescent Society crews rushed victims to hospitals but were delayed by Israeli gates and roadblocks on the way to the town, leaving the victims without medical care for crucial minutes. Palestinians in the occupied West Bank lack the shelters and sirens most Israelis have access to and have used to keep safe during the war.
articles.title: Palestinians were bystanders to the Iran war. Now they're victims too

articles.author: Jon Gambrell, David Rising, Mike Corder, Natalie Melzer
articles.description: Iran has intensified its attacks on oil and gas facilities around the Gulf, dramatically raising the stakes in a war that is sending shock waves through the global economy. Thursday’s strikes came in retaliation for an Israeli attack on a key Iranian natural gas field. They sent fuel prices soaring and risked drawing Iran’s Arab neighbors directly into the conflict. Tehran targeted energy production, further stressing global supplies already under pressure because of Iran’s stranglehold on the Strait of Hormuz. That’s a strategic waterway through which a fifth of the world’s oil is transported. Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu says Iran no longer can enrich uranium or make ballistic missiles.
articles.title: Netanyahu says Iran can no longer enrich uranium

articles.author: Sam Metz
articles.description: Israel has killed a string of senior Iranian leaders in airstrikes, aiming to weaken or topple the Islamic Republic. But past targeted killing campaigns show such tactics have limits. Hamas and Hezbollah have survived the loss of several top figures. Experts say the effects of killing leaders are most often fleeting and it rarely addresses underlying grievances. Targeted killings also risk turning slain leaders into martyrs with enduring influence.Iran’s layered institutions have so far absorbed losses, with a new supreme leader in place and attacks ongoing.
articles.title: Israel is killing off Iran's leaders but the war rages on

articles.author: Aamer Madhani, Will Weissert
articles.description: President Donald Trump and Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu’s diverging language on Israel’s decision to attack a critical Iranian gas field marks the most notable difference of opinion between the two leaders since the start of the war against Iran. The attack by Israel on the South Pars gas field prompted Iran to retaliate against energy infrastructure in other Middle East countries. The Iranian strikes led to already elevated global energy prices further surging and spurred Gulf allies to call for Trump to rein in Netanyahu. It left Trump and Netanyahu facing questions on whether they’re entirely in sync in prosecuting the war.
articles.title: What Trump and Netanyahu's split on Iran gas field means for the war

articles.author: Lisa Mascaro, Kevin Freking
articles.description: A senior administration official says the Pentagon is seeking $200 billion in additional funds for the Iran war. The department sent the request to the White House, according to the official, who spoke on condition of anonymity to discuss the private information. It’s an extraordinarily high number and comes on top of extra funding the Defense Department already received last year in President Donald Trump’s big tax cuts bill. Asked about the figure at a press conference Thursday, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth did not directly confirm the amount, saying it could change. Congress is bracing for a new spending request but it is unclear it would have support. Trump says it's a small price to pay for security.
articles.title: Pentagon seeks $200 billion in extra funds for the Iran war, AP source says

articles.author: David A. Lieb
articles.description: Proposals to prohibit federal immigration agents from wearing masks are gaining new life in states. Washington state's Democratic governor, Bob Ferguson, is to sign legislation Thursday limiting facial coverings on law enforcement officers. This comes barely a month after a federal judge blocked a California mask ban as unlawfully discriminatory because it applied to federal officers but not state personnel. Washington state's new measure seeks to avoid that by applying to federal, state and local officers. Trump's administration says it will not abide by the Washington law. Similar bills passed recently in Oregon and Virginia, and bills have cleared at least one legislative chamber in Hawaii, Maryland and Vermont.
articles.title: States seek to unmask federal immigration agents

articles.author: Tim Sullivan
articles.description: An immigration judge has denied the asylum claim of the family of Liam Conejo Ramos, the 5-year-old boy photographed in a bunny hat and Spider-Man backpack as he was detained with his father during the immigration crackdown that shook Minneapolis earlier this year, a family lawyer said. They were ordered deported to Ecuador. The boy and his father, Adrian Conejo Arias, who is from Ecuador, were taken into custody in a Minneapolis suburb on Jan. 20 and held for 10 days in a Texas detention center before a judge ordered them released. The family’s lawyers are appealing the ruling by Judge John Burns.
articles.title: Asylum claim denied for family of the boy in a bunny hat

articles.author: Meg Kinnard
articles.description: Markwayne Mullin has moved a step closer to becoming President Donald Trump’s next homeland security secretary. A Senate committee on Thursday narrowly advanced the nomination of the Republican senator from Oklahoma. The 8-7 vote came after a contentious hearing Wednesday and sent the Cabinet nomination to the full Senate, which could act to confirm Mullin next week. That vote included a “no” from the Republican chairman, Rand Paul of Kentucky, and a “yes” from a Democrat, John Fetterman of Pennsylvania. The approval comes at a time when the parties are fighting bitterly over the policies of the Department of Homeland Security, leading to a funding lapse that is now in its 34th day.
articles.title: Mullin's DHS nomination advances to full Senate after narrow committee vote

articles.author: Kevin Freking
articles.description: There's a small sign of progress in efforts to end the Homeland Security shutdown. A bipartisan group of senators on Thursday met behind closed doors with White House border czar Tom Homan. It showed a renewed desire to end the Department of Homeland Security shutdown that began more than a month ago. Funding for the department lapsed on Feb. 14 after Democrats refused to fund Immigration and Customs Enforcement and Customs and Border Protection without changes to their enforcement operations. Sen. Katie Britt of Alabama says the next step will be another White House counteroffer and then lawmakers will regroup.
articles.title: Lamakers and White House officials meet to tackle DHS shutdown
